Irrelevant of company size, it is important you understand your company activity generates greenhouse gas emissions, which can contribute to climate change.\n

One of the major steps needed for a company to understand what its carbon footprint is will be to measure it, this can only be done using a step by step approach, documenting this within a company program. A major advantage of measuring emissions is that it can help make savings throughout the business.
The program should identify where energy is utilized, what it costs and are there alternatives available to reduce things like air emissions, sourcing raw materials and the impact on the environment this has and can your business use recycled or more environmentally friendly alternatives.
Example: Excessive energy usage and high spend on utilities to heat and cool the company administrative office can be changed by installing high-efficiency rooftop heating and cooling units. Brought about a reduction in 1000’s of tonnes of CO2 and potentially hundreds of thousands from the bottom line.
Your clients, customers and potential investors expect you to act in a more responsible manner, making changes impacts on the environment and improve human wellbeing. Governments are introducing laws and starting to tax companies based on their emissions, having the program will ensure your business can demonstrate compliance in this area.
Does the company currently have a Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions measurement program?​
Yes, and the company has policies to incentivize executive members to meet reduction targets​
Yes, and the company has set reduction targets​
Yes​
No, but the company plans to implement a program in the next 12 months​
No, the company does not have a program in place​

It is essential that all companies irrelevant of their size, business activity and where they are based in the world provide a safe work environment for all their workers including any subcontractors, or others employed by other companies but may be impacted by the actions of your company.\n

When accidents happen in the work environment, it is essential that a record of the occurrence is made. In some countries it is a legal mandate that a record is documented and in some jurisdictions the company may have to report the numbers to a government agency or insurance company.
\nMaking a record allows other parties to make an assessment of the companies safety performance, their reporting culture and also gives the opportunity to investigate what happened in the hope of preventing any form of reoccurrence of a similar accident. There are different types of injuries and these can vary in nature, they can be something as simple as a small cut or trip through to a debilitating injury. By having a system that records and tracks this information the company can use the information to analyze lagging indicators and understand where the business needs to improve performance.
\nHow the information is tracked should be in line with the business size and activity, and retrievable as some clients may ask to see this information to gain some understanding of how the company deals with this type of information. When fatalities occur, in the majority of cases it has to be reported to a government agency as well as other parties such as insurance companies and legal counsel. The process for recording and investigating should be in place and again this type of information may be requested by government representatives responsible for workplace fatalities.

Does the company have a system in place to track:

1. The number of fatalities as a result of work related injury;​
2. The number of high consequence, work related injuries (excluding fatalities); and
3. The number of work related injuries?
Yes, and the company has policies to incentivize executive members to meet goals​
Yes, and the company has goals to reduce injuries/fatalities​
Yes​
No, but the company plans to start tracking in the next 12 months​
No​

Monitoring and tracking diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) has many benefits it is more than simply having policies and statements, it requires record keeping and monitoring certain employment practices such as recruitment, promotion and how people are paid.

Tracking DEI metrics provides transparency on how the workforce is managed, how promotions are applied, is it done fairly across all genders, are workers paid differently for doing the same role, are some areas of the business subject to poor worker retention and are some workers paid differently for doing the same job, analyzing of poor retention of workers and does the recruitment process reflect the local community.
\nWhen measuring metrics, it provides transparency around how the company applies fair treatment and equal opportunity for all workers, removes any prejudice and discrimination based on characteristics such as race, gender, sexual orientation, nationality or religion (Protected characteristics). Other metrics can include confirming the workforce is reflective of the local community, transparency on the gender breakdown of the senior management team e.g.,, more males than females and compensation and benefits are applied fairly, recruitment practice is it the same for all vacancies and retention rates of workers are analyzed.
Does the company track diversity, equity, and inclusion metrics?
Yes, and the company has policies for fostering equitable employee representation
Yes​
No, but the company plans to start tracking within the next 12 months
No​

Many think the issue of slavery does not happen in the workplace any more or that child labor only happens in poor, developing economies. It is a sad fact that slavery and underage labor takes place in all countries around the world.\n

In modern times there are in excess of 40 million people currently in some form of forced labor in the world. While it may not happen in your company, it is possible that it could happen in your supply chain either directly entering your worksites or involved in the manufacture or sourcing of materials and products, for example, it is a fact that children have been found in the production of building materials and agriculture in Asia, modern slaves have been found in companies in developed countries throughout the world.
\nWith climate change and an increased number of people trying to escape wars and civil conflict increases the chances of people being exploited. We are seeing governments around the world now making it a legal requirement to address the issue of slavery and they are required to demonstrate how they are working to minimize slavery within their business operation, this can be in the form of a policy, management procedures and other controls such as due diligence checks, independent audits and mandating strategic suppliers also conduct checks on the lower levels of the supply chain.
Does the company have policies in place to prevent against child labor and forced or compulsory labor?
Yes - TBD (Asking suppliers)​
Yes​
No, but the company plans to implement policy within the next 12 months​
No​

All companies irrelevant of size or business activity would benefit from conducting financial risk impact assessments, this confirms how debt is managed, financial obligations are covered, defaulting on a loan, delays in the delivery of a contract and how the company would deal with natural disasters e.g., flooding.\n

Other financial impacts include loss of power, international events impacting on the company being able to source goods or materials due to weather, political instability and sudden increases in material costs.
A materiality assessment is where you identify and assess potential environmental impacts focused on environmental, social and governance issues and how they can impact shareholders and stakeholders. Findings are documented and used to define how the company sets business targets, meets corporate indicators and if the issues are negative to the business.
Example: Materials being sourced for the delivery of a project are found to be sourced from a protected environment, the cleaning and processing was conducted by child labor. There was no supplier due diligence or verification to ensure the contract conditions were addressed and the problem was picked up by a pressure group and a human rights organization who have made the information public.
The assessment is a process where the company can engage with stakeholders, shareholders and consumers to understand what standards they demand when investing in or buying the services of the business. Once potential issues have been identified, appropriate action and rectification plans can be implemented and confirm how these will be communicated. The issues can have a direct or indirect impact on how the business grows economically and companies of all sizes can benefit from the assessment.
In the last three years, has the company conducted a financial impact or materiality risk assessment to determine which ESG issues affect the company's business performance?​
Yes​​
No, but the company plans to conduct a materiality analysis within the next 12 months​
No​

Biofuel is derived from living materials such as plant based feedstocks, producing products such as: Ethanol, Biodiesel and Biobutanol. The biofuels industry relies on plant-based feedstocks.\n

To ensure consistent supply of feedstocks, companies need to ensure agriculture practices minimize the potential for negative impacts to the environment, for example: Excessive use of water, reduction in soil quality and erosion, increased use of pesticides, etc.
\nTo assist in identifying preferred/most effective sources for biofuel feedstocks, third-party assessment and certification are available.
\nThe assessment should include minimization of deforestation, biodiversity loss, soil degradation, and water pollution.
Does the company use a third-party (certification or industry body) environmental standard to certify biofuel production?
Yes, and the company publicly disclosed the feedstock impacts and has set reduction targets
Yes, and the company has quantified the environmental impacts of feedstocks with third-party certification
Yes
No, but the company plans to implement a strategy in the next 12 months
No
With global focus on increasing biofuel production as a measure to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, comprehensive understanding of the entire supply chain and its emissions are critical.\n

Furthermore, many governmental policies worldwide set the fossil-fuel baseline necessary for biofuels to qualify for advanced biofuel recognition.
\nThe greenhouse gas calculation for biofuel supply chains should include both direct and indirect emissions from feedstock crop production and corresponding land use, fuel refining, and both feedstock and fuel transport.
Does the company have greenhouse gas emissions for each stage in the life cycle quantified, by fuel type?
Yes, and the company publicly discloses GHG emissions across the lifecycle, by fuel type, and has set reduction targets
Yes, and the company has captured GHG across the lifecycle, by fuel type
Yes
No, but the company plans to implement a strategy in the next 12 months
No

Companies that are not perceived as being conservative with their use of water, addressing its use, quality and sourcing can be subject to negative assessment by local communities, potential investors and stakeholders, it is essential that water management and conservation is seen as a key mandate to be implemented by a company that uses water as part of their scope. This mitigates the probability of facilitating reputational risk issues.\n
\n

Additionally, companies face operational and regulatory risks due to water scarcity, costs of water acquisition, regulations on effluents or amount of water used, and competition with local communities and other industries for limited water resources. Risks are higher in regions of water scarcity, due to potential water availability constraints and price volatility.
\nCompanies that are unable to secure a stable water supply could face production disruptions, while rising water prices could directly increase production costs. Adoption of technologies and processes that reduce water consumption could lower operating risks and costs for companies by minimizing the impact of regulations, water supply shortages, and community-related disruptions on company operations.
\nWater efficiency is defined as the minimization of the amount of water used to accomplish a function. In simple terms, it means doing more with less water. This includes water recycled, defined as, any volume of water reused multiple times.
Does the company have a water management program that addresses baseline water stress?
Yes, and the company publicly disclosed their water basin impacts and mitigation strategy
Yes, and the company has identified all operations in high or extremely high water stress regions
Yes
No, but the company plans to implement a strategy in the next 12 months
No

\nAssessing the risk in the supply chain through engagement and/or third-party certification is an important consideration for biofuels producers.

It is essential that if your company generates or works with any form of hazardous waste irrelevant of how big the company is, it should develop and implement a hazardous waste program. It should be used to help protect the health and safety of people (Not just your workers), minimize the potential impact it could have on the environment both during production, storage and eventual disposal.\n
\n

Throughout the developed world there are regulations and laws that have been developed to manage hazardous waste. Your plan should reflect how you address these requirements.
Controls and plans are essential when sourcing from emerging countries where legislation and application of good practice may not be prevalent.
The waste plan should cover as a minimum the following areas: • A scope , identifying what waste is covered, where it comes from • Labelling especially if it is being shipped to other countries • Storage, quarantining and transportation (All modes) • Inspection program (Safety, waste analysis, sampling, etc.) • Training (Handling, emergencies, spills, drills, different waste recognition, contingencies, etc.) • Working with government agencies, formal reporting • Disposal, neutralization and validation of disposal process
Does the company have a waste management program?
Yes, and the company has publicly stated reduction goals
Yes, and waste disposed, by type and method, are disclosed publicly
Yes
No, but the company plans to implement a strategy in the next 12 months
No

Supply chain quality is essential to protecting consumer health and corporate value.\n

Many sectors have spent many years trying to understand the impact their work activity and that of their supply chains has on the local and global environment. Many companies look at what is termed a “Product life cycle”, an assessment is made at each stage of the manufacturing process, product implementation, maintenance, renewal and ultimately its disposal.\n
\n

Looking at life cycles relating to products and their impacts, analysis can lead to opportunities to reduce raw material consumption, optimize transportation and recycle materials at the end of their useful life.
\nThis supports a circular economy where items are repurposed or recycled into new useful products, thereby reducing reliance on virgin raw materials.
\nClients, stakeholders, consumer pressure and government applying legislation are pushing for greater innovation needed to look at all stages of the product life cycle from placing the first order, mandating the best environmental option through to the eventual position of dealing with the product once it has no more viable use.
Does the company have a product recall, take-back, and/or disposal program for patients?
Yes, and the company has targets to improve its take-back performance
Yes, and the company publicly discloses details on its take-back program
Yes
No, but the company plans to implement a strategy in the next 12 months
No

Clients are now starting to look at suppliers who are actively trying to reduce their energy use and reduce their emissions when producing goods or sourcing materials. To validate these reductions, the company should develop an energy plan showing where and how savings can be made\n

The energy management plan should identify the quantity and sources of energy (including type) consumed and opportunities to improve energy efficiency at all stages of the production process or when delivering the work scope (e.g., Non-manufacturers can switch to energy saving plant and equipment, change lighting in the factory, use electric vehicles, etc.).
\nFossil fuel based energy sources emit more greenhouse gases than renewable energy sources like solar, hydroelectric, or wind energy. Identifying the sources of energy used allows the opportunity to change the energy mix.
Does the company have an energy management program that identifies opportunity to evaluate the company's energy efficiency and mix?
Yes, and the company has targets to reduce energy consumption and increase renewable energy use
Yes, and the company publicly reports its energy consumption, by type
Yes
No, but the company plans to implement a strategy in the next 12 months

It is possible that certain products that are found in the workplace or used to deliver company services contain chemicals. Some of these products may require a defined assessment of the risk found when used /applied in certain environments, there can be impacts to a worker’s health, individuals who are not employed by the company but can be impacted as a result of the work activity and the environment.\n

It is possible that chemicals in themselves may not be classified as hazardous but as a result of the work activity can generate risks e.g., welding fumes.
Chemicals found in products can be in liquid, solid, powder or gaseous form.
Every company will have their own method of addressing the risk and controls needed to deal with chemicals found in products.
As a minimum, client will expect you as an end user of specific chemicals to have conducted the following: • Confirm chemicals found in the product before and during use • Identify what hazards are associated with the identifiable chemicals • Confirm the environment where the products will be used and confirm they are suitable for the work environment and if required ensure the client has agreed they are appropriate for the workplace e.g. chemicals used on a chemical site may not be suitable for use in a food manufacturer • Training needed to use the chemicals safely • Who can be affected if exposed? • Emergency provisions in the event something goes wrong e.g. first-aid, fire, spills. • Transport. Storage of the products • Are they compatible with other products being used simultaneously? • Confirm the risks being assessed are conducted by competent individuals • Ensure a record is made of the risk program and contents
Does the company have a program in place to effectively assess, manage risks and minimize the exposure associated with chemicals found in products?
Yes, and the company has assessed all sites for biodiversity impact
Yes, and the company has assessed at least 50% of its sites
Yes
No, but the company plans to implement a policy in the next 12 months
No

When potential exists for workers and the work environment to be exposed to chemicals, a plan must be developed to identify contaminants, exposure methods, and risk controls.\n

The plan should identify key contaminants and the potential impact to workers, members of the public, and the environment.
For example, the monitoring plan should include testing, containment, emergency controls, and notifying emergency services for substances such as: • Nitrogen oxides (NO and NO2), • Sulphur oxides (SO2 and SO3), • Particulate matter (PM10), and • any volatile organic compounds (VOCs).
The plan should contain good hygiene practices and a hierarchy of controls to reduce exposure to its lowest level.
If it is not possible to eliminate exposure or release, the plan must document how to reduce exposure through engineering and administrative controls. As the last resort, the plan must outline the provision of personal protective equipment.
The plan should reflect changes of note in the regulatory environment and limit impacts on air quality.
Does the company have a program to manage risks associated with its air emissions of industry-specific pollutants (NOx, SOx, VOCs, and HAPs)?

Yes

Tower climbing is a highly hazardous activity and can present significant risk to workers.\n

Tower climbing risks include: Fall from height / equipment failure / poor weather /structural collapse / poor access and egress and electrical hazards. In some cases, the towers are located in inner cities or very remote locations, where risk to the personnel may not be seen as a risk factor.
